title: Chapati

overview.description: Soft, thin Indian flatbreads made from wholemeal atta flour, water and a little oil.

ingredients: Ghee or butter, to serve, optional
method: Mix the atta and salt in a large bowl.
Gradually add the warm water, mixing with your fingers until a dough forms.
Knead for 5–8 minutes until the dough is smooth and soft.
Cover and rest for at least 20 minutes, preferably 30–60 minutes.
Divide the dough into 8 equal portions and roll each into a smooth ball.
Flatten one ball and dust lightly with atta.
Roll into a thin circle approximately 15–18cm in diameter, turning and dusting lightly as needed to prevent sticking.
Heat a dry tawa or heavy frying pan over a medium-high heat.
Place a chapati in the hot pan.
Cook until small bubbles appear on the surface.
Turn and cook the other side until lightly browned in places.
Turn once more and gently press the surface with a clean cloth or spatula to encourage it to puff.
Remove and keep wrapped in a clean tea towel.
Brush with ghee or butter if desired.

learning.did-you-know: Chapati are traditionally cooked on a tawa, and the dramatic puffing happens when steam forms between the two layers of the dough.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How hydration, kneading, resting and rolling affect a simple flatbread, and how to cook chapati so they remain soft.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A simple, everyday Indian flatbread that is inexpensive, versatile and an excellent way to build confidence with dough.
chapman-tips: The dough should feel soft and slightly tacky but not wet.
Resting the dough makes it much easier to roll and helps produce softer chapati.
Use as little dry flour as possible when rolling because too much can make the cooked chapati dry.
The pan must be properly hot before cooking.
If the chapati browns immediately, the pan is too hot; if it takes a long time to develop spots, the pan is too cool.
Keep cooked chapati wrapped in a clean tea towel so they stay soft.
If the chapati do not puff, don't worry. They can still be perfectly good.
notes.batch-cooking: Cooked chapati freeze well. Cool completely, stack with baking paper between them if desired and freeze in a sealed bag or container. Reheat directly from frozen in a dry frying pan.
notes.leftovers: Use leftover chapati as wraps, cut into strips for dipping or crisp them in a frying pan to make a snack.
